title: Spinning Perendale wool
subtitle: A New Zealand cross-bred fleece with medium-fine fibre and moderate crimp
excerpt: Perendale is a New Zealand breed developed from Cheviot and Romney stock, producing a medium-fine fleece with moderate crimp and a staple length of 10 to 15 cm. This entry covers its preparation and spinning into a smooth, versatile worsted-to-aran-weight yarn.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from breed spinning practice. Perendale fibre properties documented in New Zealand Wool Board breed information and British guild spinning resources. Spinning technique synthesised from medium-staple worsted and semi-worsted methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: The natural waviness of a wool fibre. The crimp frequency and amplitude determine how springy and elastic the yarn will be. Perendale has moderate, regular crimp.
The length of an individual fleece lock from the cut end to the tip. Perendale staple typically runs 10 to 15 cm.

content.text: Perendale is a useful mid-range fleece: not as fine as Merino, not as coarse as a hill breed. The
is medium and regular, giving the finished yarn good elasticity. The
of 10 to 15 cm makes it easy to prepare by either carding or light combing. Perendale produces a strong, versatile aran-to-worsted yarn that holds its shape well in knitted fabric.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing away from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Washed Perendale fleece or commercially prepared Perendale top

content.text: For commercially prepared top, pre-draft lightly to about one and a half times the original diameter before spinning. For raw locks, card to rolags for a woollen-draw yarn (more elastic, more loft) or light-comb for a semi-worsted yarn (smoother, denser). Perendale is amenable to either approach.

Set the wheel to a medium flyer ratio (7:1 to 9:1) with moderate uptake. Perendale responds well to both the worsted draw from combed top and the woollen draw from carded rolags. Spin to a consistent aran weight; check wraps per inch every 15 minutes and adjust drafting as needed.

Ply two bobbins together from the lazy kate. Perendale two-ply at aran weight is a round, firm yarn that wears well. Set the twist in warm water, hang to dry, and the finished yarn will bloom slightly to give a soft but resilient surface.

items.problem: The finished yarn feels scratchy against the skin
items.cause: Perendale is not as fine as merino and some batches can read as scratchy at finer gauges
items.fix: Use Perendale for outerwear, bags, and household textiles where softness against the skin is less critical. For next-to-skin wear, blend with merino at 50:50 before carding.

items.problem: The singles vary in thickness across the bobbin
items.fix: Pre-draft the top more thoroughly and maintain a consistent rhythm at the wheel.
